Press enter or click to view image in full size

In the previous article, I introduced the overall architecture of a modular frontend platform built with Next.js and pnpm Workspace.
One of the most important decisions in this architecture was creating a dedicated package called platform-core.
Instead of allowing every feature module to implement authentication, networking, permissions, and shared application state independently, these responsibilities are centralized into a reusable platform layer.
This approach keeps feature modules focused on business logic while the platform provides the common infrastructure.

The Problem
Imagine an application with multiple feature modules:

• User

• Transfer

• Payment

• Notification

• Report
Without a shared platform, every module typically implements its own:

• HTTP Client

• Authentication

• Token Storage

• Permission Checking

• API Configuration

• Error Handling
Besides duplicated code, every implementation may behave slightly differently.
As the number of modules grows, maintaining consistency becomes increasingly difficult.

The Platform Core
To solve this problem, I created a dedicated package:
packages/
└── platform-coreThe package is responsible only for reusable application infrastructure.
Current capabilities include:

• Authentication

• HTTP Client

• API Layer

• Event Bus

• Permission Management

• Navigation Store

• Shared Types
Feature modules never communicate directly with each other.
Instead, they consume services exposed by platform-core.
Project Structure
platform-core/src/
├── auth/
├── event-bus/
├── navigation/
├── network/
├── permission/
├── store/
├── types/
└── index.ts
Every feature imports from a single entry point.
import {
login,
http,
useAuthStore,
eventBus
} from "@company/platform-core";This keeps dependencies clean and hides internal implementation details.

Shared Authentication
Authentication is implemented once and shared across every module.
Instead of storing user information inside individual pages, the platform exposes a global Zustand store.
const user = useAuthStore(state => state.user);The authentication service is also centralized.
await login({
username,
password
});Once authentication succeeds, the platform stores:

• User information

• Access token

• Refresh token
Every feature automatically receives the updated session without additional configuration.
HTTP Client
Networking is another responsibility owned by the platform.
Rather than exposing Axios directly, feature modules consume a simplified HTTP client.
const users = await http.get<User[]>("/users");The platform becomes the single place for:

• Base URL configuration

• Authorization headers

• Token injection

• Request interceptors

• Response interceptors

• Error normalization
This abstraction makes it possible to replace Axios in the future without affecting feature modules.
Centralized Error Handling
Instead of exposing raw Axios errors, the platform converts them into application-specific errors.
For example:
try {
await login(payload);
} catch (error) {
if (error instanceof ApiError) {
console.log(error.message);
}
}Feature modules no longer need to understand Axios internals.
They only work with platform-defined error types.
Permission Management
Permissions are stored together with the authenticated user.
const { has } = usePermission();if (has("TRANSFER")) {
// Render Transfer Module
}Because permission checking is centralized, navigation, buttons, and pages all use the same authorization logic.
Navigation Store
Navigation is also shared through the platform.
Instead of coupling modules to routing logic, a shared navigation store controls which module is currently active.
navigate("transfer");The Dashboard Shell simply reacts to navigation state changes.
This keeps routing logic separated from feature implementation.
Event Bus
One of the most useful services inside the platform is the Event Bus.
Instead of importing functions from other modules, communication happens through events.
eventBus.emit("LOGIN", user);eventBus.emit("NOTIFICATION", {
type: "success",
message: "Transfer completed"
});Modules subscribe only to the events they care about.
useEvent("LOGIN", user => {
console.log(user.firstName);
});This approach significantly reduces coupling between modules and prepares the architecture for future Micro Frontend adoption.

Why a Dedicated Platform Layer?
Creating a Platform Core offers several advantages:

• Single implementation for shared capabilities

• Consistent authentication and networking

• Reduced code duplication

• Easier maintenance

• Better separation between infrastructure and business logic

• Simpler onboarding for new feature modules
Most importantly, feature modules remain focused on solving business problems instead of rebuilding application infrastructure.

What’s Next?
The Platform Core provides the foundation, but another challenge remains:
How can feature modules communicate without depending on each other?
In the next article, I’ll take a deeper look at the Event Bus implementation, explain why I chose an event-driven approach, and show how it enables loose coupling between independently developed modules.
